policy_statement: Economically valorise biomass by installing intermunicipal systems for collecting and interim storage of biomass residues from forestry and agricultural activities in rural areas.
mechanism_description: It installs intermunicipal collection and interim storage systems for forestry and agricultural biomass residues so the biomass can be used productively, helping reduce emissions compared with unmanaged residue disposal and more fossil-intensive alternatives.
primary_causal_pathway: Biomass residue management change → ↑ biomass utilized / ↓ unmanaged decay or burning + ↓ fossil energy/material use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in activity/system: Municipal/regional governments and partners build and operate intermunicipal systems to collect, aggregate, and temporarily store forestry and agricultural residues that would otherwise be scattered, burned, or left to decompose unmanaged.  
2) Mediating physical process: Centralized handling enables controlled end-uses (e.g., bioenergy with efficient combustion, bioproducts) and reduces uncontrolled aerobic/anaerobic decomposition or open burning; it also enables substitution away from fossil fuels/materials.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions (avoided methane and black carbon from unmanaged disposal/burning where applicable, plus avoided fossil CO₂ through substitution), contingent on sustainable sourcing and efficient conversion pathways.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced net GHG emissions by diverting biomass residues into controlled productive use that displaces fossil-intensive alternatives and/or avoids emissions from unmanaged residue disposal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core is enabling biomass residues to be used in ways that reduce net GHGs relative to baseline (unmanaged residue disposal and/or fossil-intensive alternatives).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it improves material/energy utilization (resource efficiency) and can reduce wildfire risk locally, the largest and most direct climate-relevant effect across implementations is emissions mitigation via avoided decomposition/burning emissions and fossil displacement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If residues are used inefficiently, transported long distances, or sourced unsustainably, benefits can erode; however, the mechanism as stated targets emissions reduction through productive utilization. Any adaptation co-benefits (e.g., fuel-load reduction) are secondary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that builds collection + interim storage to enable residue biomass utilization is fundamentally aimed at changing residue fate and substituting for higher-emitting disposal/energy pathways, which is mitigation-dominant.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the mitigation occurs mainly through fossil fuel substitution, avoided open burning, avoided landfill/decomposition methane, or wildfire-risk reduction depends on policy-specific end-use and baseline evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could shift toward Resource Efficiency if the primary documented outcome is “same service with less virgin material/energy” without clear GHG substitution claims; could include Adaptation co-benefits where residue removal is primarily for wildfire fuel-load management. Net mitigation depends on conversion efficiency, counterfactual residue fate, transport distances, and sustainability of biomass sourcing.
key_indicators: “installation of Intermunicipal Systems”; “collection and interim storage”; “biomass left over from forestry and agricultural activities”; “Economic valorisation of biomass”
co_benefits: Potential wildfire risk reduction (fuel load reduction), rural economic development/job creation, improved waste/residue management and local air quality (if avoiding open burning)
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit mandate, subsidy, or standard is stated—classification rests on “installation” indicating capital investment in collection/storage infrastructure; wildfire/air-quality benefits are plausible for Portugal but not directly specified in the text.
